steps.action: Oro, also known as horo and hora, is a type of c with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Oro, also known as horo and hora, is a type of c with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The original meaning of the Greek word χορός may
steps.text: The original meaning of the Greek word χορός may have been "circle".

steps.action: Also, the words hora and oro are found in many S
steps.text: Also, the words hora and oro are found in many Slavic languages and have the meaning of "round (dance)"; the verb oriti means "to speak, sound, sing" and previously meant "to celebrate".

steps.action: The Khorumi dance of Georgia also might be conne
steps.text: The Khorumi dance of Georgia also might be connected to the Horon dance in the neighboring Turkish regions, as it rose out of the Adjara region, where Kartvelian Laz people co-existed for centuries with Greek Pontians.

steps.action: The dance is usually accompanied by musical inst
steps.text: The dance is usually accompanied by musical instruments such as the cymbalom, accordion, violin, viola, double bass, saxophone, trumpet or the pan pipes.

steps.action: Some of the biggest hora circles can be found on
steps.text: Some of the biggest hora circles can be found on early 20th century movies filmed by the Manaki brothers in Pindus, Greece, and performed by local Aromanians.

steps.action: It is not necessary to be in a circle; a curving
steps.text: It is not necessary to be in a circle; a curving line of people is also acceptable.

steps.action: The steps used in a horo dance are extremely div
steps.text: The steps used in a horo dance are extremely diverse.

steps.action: The horo may vary between three and seven or eig
steps.text: The horo may vary between three and seven or eight steps forward and one to five or six steps back, depending on the specific type.

steps.action: They differ by the rhythm of the music and the s
steps.text: They differ by the rhythm of the music and the steps taken.

steps.action: There are no two horo dances with similar steps.
steps.text: There are no two horo dances with similar steps.

steps.action: There are probably over one hundred types of hor
steps.text: There are probably over one hundred types of horo dances in the Bulgarian folklore.

steps.action: In the past, the horo dance had a social role in
steps.text: In the past, the horo dance had a social role in Bulgarian society.

steps.action: It was mainly for fun, as a contest of skills, o
steps.text: It was mainly for fun, as a contest of skills, or for show, leading to the development of the variety of horo dances.

steps.action: There are hora for people with little skill that
steps.text: There are hora for people with little skill that can be learned in five to ten minutes, but there are also very sophisticated dances that cannot be learned unless one is fluent in many of the simpler dances.

steps.action: The origins of the word oro vary from its use in
steps.text: The origins of the word oro vary from its use in socializing and celebrating to historical dancing before going into battle.

steps.action: The oro is danced in a circle, with men and wome
steps.text: The oro is danced in a circle, with men and women holding one another by hand.

steps.action: Roma Horos, and Roma music in general, are very
steps.text: Roma Horos, and Roma music in general, are very much appreciated among the non-Roma in the Balkans, as they also have a reputation as skillful performers of other people's folk music.

steps.action: Among Yiddish-speaking Jews, the triple-meter ho
steps.text: Among Yiddish-speaking Jews, the triple-meter horah has also been called zhok (Romanian joc, 'dance') or krumer tants (Yiddish: 'crooked dance').

steps.action: Although horah was culturally adopted from South
steps.text: Although horah was culturally adopted from Southeast Europe, it became even more popular with the Jewish diaspora due to the Hora Agadati dance.

steps.action: In 1924, the dancer and choreographer Baruch Aga
steps.text: In 1924, the dancer and choreographer Baruch Agadati choreographed a dance based on the Romanian Hora dance that became known as "Hora Agadati".

steps.action: It was performed by the Ohel Workers' Theatre, w
steps.text: It was performed by the Ohel Workers' Theatre, which toured pioneer settlements in the Jezreel Valley.

steps.action: According to Gurit Kadman, the original melody w
steps.text: According to Gurit Kadman, the original melody was a Moldavian folk tune (Baruch Agadati was born in the Bessarabia Governorate) and Gurit asked Uriya Boskovitz to write a new one.

steps.action: Now the dance is usually performed to Israeli fo
steps.text: Now the dance is usually performed to Israeli folk songs, and sometimes to Jewish songs, often to the music of "Hava Nagila".
